content.text: An Azure subscription is the fundamental billing and governance boundary. Deciding how to distribute workloads across subscriptions — one subscription for everything, or many subscriptions separated by environment or workload — is one of the most consequential design choices in Azure architecture. The right strategy depends on organisation size, compliance requirements, blast-radius tolerance, and operational maturity.

attrs.alt: Three subscription strategies: single subscription, per-environment, and per-workload

content.text: A subscription is more than a billing unit. It provides several isolation boundaries that resource groups do not:
Quota isolation: resource quotas (VM cores, public IPs, storage accounts) are per-subscription. Workload A consuming its quota in a shared subscription can block Workload B from scaling.
Blast-radius containment: a misconfigured resource group delete policy or a runaway automation script can at most affect the resources in one subscription.
Policy segmentation: management group policy inheritance applies at the subscription level. Different subscriptions can be placed in different management groups with different policy sets.
Cost attribution clarity: subscription-level cost reports separate workload costs without relying on tag-based filtering (which depends on consistent tagging).
Service limits: some Azure services have per-subscription limits (e.g., 150 VNets, 25 ExpressRoute circuits). Separate subscriptions avoid these limits interacting across unrelated workloads.

A resource group is an organisational boundary but not an isolation boundary. Network traffic, RBAC inheritance, policy compliance, and quotas all cross resource group boundaries freely. Use subscriptions when you need genuine isolation.

One subscription for all workloads, environments, and teams. Resource groups provide the only organisational structure. This is suitable for small organisations or early-stage projects where overhead must be minimal.
No compliance requirement mandating environment isolation
Development and production traffic volumes are both small (quota limits are not a concern)
Single team with unified cost responsibility
A dev team's destructive test (`az group delete`) risks production resources in the same subscription
Quota usage from load testing in the dev environment limits production autoscaling
Policy requirements differ between environments (e.g., dev allows public IPs, production does not) — these cannot be expressed with a single subscription in a single management group
Chargeback to different cost centres requires tag-based filtering — which breaks when resources are untagged

Separate subscriptions for production and non-production (development, test, staging). All production workloads share the production subscription; all development and testing activity shares the non-production subscription. This is the most common pattern for small-to-medium enterprises.

Corp Management Group
├── Corp-Production MG
│   └── Subscription: corp-prod-001
│       ├── rg-finance-prod
│       ├── rg-engineering-prod
│       └── rg-shared-prod
└── Corp-NonProd MG
    └── Subscription: corp-nonprod-001
        ├── rg-finance-dev
        ├── rg-engineering-dev
        └── rg-shared-dev
Policy enforcement differs between environments: production has deny-public-IP; non-production permits it for developer accessibility
Quota separation: a load test consuming 200 VM cores in non-production does not block production autoscaling
Cost attribution is clear at the billing level: production costs vs. development costs are separate without tagging
RBAC is easier: developers have Contributor on the non-production subscription; on production they have only Reader (with deployments handled by CI/CD pipelines)

# Assuming subscriptions are already created via EA/MCA portal or subscription vending
PROD_SUB='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
NONPROD_SUB='11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111'

# Place in the correct management group tiers
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name 'mg-corp-prod' \
  --subscription $PROD_SUB

az account management-group subscription add \
  --name 'mg-corp-nonprod' \
  --subscription $NONPROD_SUB

# Grant developers Contributor on non-prod (read-only on prod)
DEV_GROUP_OID='aaaaaaaa-aaaa-aaaa-aaaa-aaaaaaaaaaaa'

az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $DEV_GROUP_OID \
  --role 'Contributor' \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$NONPROD_SUB"

az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $DEV_GROUP_OID \
  --role 'Reader' \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$PROD_SUB"

Each distinct workload (or business unit) gets its own set of subscriptions — at minimum one for production and one for non-production. This is the pattern recommended by the Cloud Adoption Framework for organisations with compliance requirements, large teams, or workloads with significantly different governance needs.

Corp Management Group
├── Corp-Production MG
│   ├── Subscription: finance-prod-001
│   │   ├── rg-finance-app-prod
│   │   └── rg-finance-db-prod
│   └── Subscription: engineering-prod-001
│       ├── rg-eng-api-prod
│       └── rg-eng-frontend-prod
└── Corp-NonProd MG
    ├── Subscription: finance-dev-001
    └── Subscription: engineering-dev-001
Regulatory isolation: PCI-DSS cardholder data must be isolated in a subscription where no non-PCI workloads run
Independent quota pools: a data-intensive workload needs 500+ VM cores without affecting other workloads
Separate RBAC ownership: Finance manages their own subscription; Engineering manages theirs — no shared admin model
Different cost centre chargeback: each subscription maps directly to a cost centre code in the ERP system
Independent deployment velocity: Finance can have a conservative change window; Engineering can deploy continuously

The default limit is 1,000 subscriptions per Azure AD tenant (can be raised to 10,000 by support request). With a per-workload strategy and 50 workloads, 4 environments (dev/test/staging/prod) each generates 200 subscriptions. Plan the naming convention and subscription lifecycle management before scaling.

# List all subscriptions in the tenant with their management group parent
az graph query -q "
  ResourceContainers
  | where type == 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
  | extend MGParent = tostring(properties.managementGroupAncestorsChain[0].name)
  | project SubName=name, SubId=subscriptionId, MGParent, State=properties.state
  | order by MGParent asc, SubName asc
" --query data -o table

# Count total subscriptions
az account list --all --query 'length(@)' -o tsv

A consistent naming convention is essential when managing many subscriptions. The name should encode the workload, environment, and region (if region-specific) so operators can identify a subscription from CLI output or cost reports without looking up metadata.
Distinguishes tenant subscriptions in multi-tenant views
Use purpose, not team name (teams change)
Allows multiple subscriptions per workload-env pair without renaming

# Examples following the pattern: <org>-<workload>-<env>-<seq>
# contoso-finance-prod-001
# contoso-finance-dev-001
# contoso-platform-connectivity-prod-001
# contoso-platform-management-prod-001
# contoso-eng-prod-001
# contoso-eng-dev-001

# Tag subscriptions for additional metadata (cost centre, owner, workload ID)
PROD_SUB='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
az tag update \
  --resource-id "/subscriptions/$PROD_SUB" \
  --operation merge \
  --tags environment=production workload=finance owner=cfo@contoso.com cost-centre=FIN-001
Yes — required for cardholder data isolation
Per-workload billing to separate cost centres
Terraform: Automating Subscription Creation and Placement

Subscription vending at scale requires automating both subscription provisioning and management group placement. The following Terraform uses the azurerm provider's subscription resource (available since provider 3.x) alongside management group association to fully codify the strategy.

Declaring subscriptions as Terraform resources makes the full subscription lifecycle — create, rename, move, cancel — part of a GitOps workflow. Peer-reviewed PRs become the audit trail for every subscription provisioning decision.
Enforcing Strategy Boundaries With Azure Policy

Subscription strategy is only as durable as the guardrails enforcing it. Azure Policy applied at management group level ensures that resources created in a subscription conform to the governance tier of the management group they inhabit — regardless of which team provisioned the subscription.

# Assign an Allowed Locations policy at the Corp-Prod MG to enforce that
# production workloads only deploy to approved regions

CORP_PROD_MG='/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp-prod'

az policy assignment create \
  --name 'corp-prod-allowed-regions' \
  --display-name 'Corp-Prod: Allowed Regions' \
  --policy 'e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4f' \
  --scope $CORP_PROD_MG \
  --params '{"listOfAllowedLocations":{"value":["eastus","eastus2","westeurope"]}}'

# Assign a policy requiring specific tags on all new subscriptions placed in Corp
CORP_MG='/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp'

az policy assignment create \
  --name 'require-cost-center-tag' \
  --display-name 'Require cost-center tag on resources' \
  --policy '1e30110a-5ceb-460c-a204-c1c3969c6d62' \
  --scope $CORP_MG \
  --params '{"tagName":{"value":"cost-center"}}'

# List all policy assignments inherited by a subscription
SUB_ID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
az policy assignment list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query '[].{Name:displayName,Scope:scope,Effect:parameters.effect.value}' \
  -o table

When an organisation has dozens or hundreds of subscriptions, consistent naming is not optional — it is the primary discovery mechanism. A structured naming convention encodes environment, workload, and purpose directly into the subscription display name, making subscriptions self-describing in the Azure Portal, in billing exports, and in Resource Graph queries.

# Recommended display name patterns (displayed in Azure Portal and billing)
# Pattern: <env>-<workload>[-<suffix>]

# Per-workload strategy (one sub per workload per env)
prod-payments
nonprod-payments
prod-identity
nonprod-identity
sandbox-jane-smith       # individual sandbox, include owner

# Single-subscription strategy (small orgs)
corp-primary             # one sub for all corporate workloads

# Per-environment strategy (one sub per environment)
prod-primary
staging-primary
dev-primary

# Verify all subscriptions follow the naming pattern using Azure Resource Graph
az graph query -q "
  ResourceContainers
  | where type == 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
  | where name !matches regex @'^(prod|nonprod|staging|dev|sandbox)-[a-z0-9-]+'
  | project name, subscriptionId
" --query 'data[]' -o table

Most organisations start with a single-subscription or per-environment strategy and migrate toward per-workload as they grow. Migration requires moving subscriptions between management groups, updating naming, and reconciling RBAC and policy assignments that were designed for the old structure.
Inventory all resources in the subscription to be split — use Resource Graph to identify workload boundaries
Create the new target subscriptions in the correct management group (Terraform apply of the new subscription resources)
Migrate resources using Azure Resource Mover or az resource move for compatible resource types
Update RBAC: revoke broad assignments inherited from the old parent MG, create workload-scoped assignments at the new MG
Validate cost allocation: confirm tags and budget alerts are configured on the new subscriptions
Decommission the source subscription by removing all resources, then cancelling it

# Step 1: inventory resources in the subscription to be split
OLD_SUB='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
az graph query -q "
  Resources
  | where subscriptionId == '$OLD_SUB'
  | summarize count() by resourceGroup, type
  | order by resourceGroup asc
" -o table

# Step 2: after creating new subs, move a resource group to a new subscription
NEW_SUB_PAYMENTS='11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111'
az resource move \
  --destination-subscription-id $NEW_SUB_PAYMENTS \
  --destination-resource-group rg-payments \
  --ids $(az group show -n rg-payments --subscription $OLD_SUB --query id -o tsv) \
  --verbose

# Step 3: verify the resource group landed in the correct subscription
az group show \
  --name rg-payments \
  --subscription $NEW_SUB_PAYMENTS \
  --query '{RG:name,Sub:id}' -o json

Each subscription carries hard limits that may force a per-workload split earlier than organisational growth alone would demand. Understanding the most common subscription limits helps teams decide when a shared subscription is no longer viable.
Single sub: VNet sprawl from many workloads approaches the limit quickly
Per-env strategy: all environments share the public IP pool
Governance-heavy orgs hit this when assigning many custom policies per subscription
High-churn environments (CI/CD ephemeral RGs) can exhaust this
High-throughput deployments may queue; separate subs alleviate contention

# Check current resource counts in a subscription against subscription limits
SUB='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'

# VNet count
az network vnet list --subscription $SUB --query 'length(@)' -o tsv

# Resource group count
az group list --subscription $SUB --query 'length(@)' -o tsv

# Policy assignment count at subscription scope
az policy assignment list --subscription $SUB \
  --query "length([?scope=='/subscriptions/$SUB'])" -o tsv

# Total resource count by type (top 10 by volume)
az graph query -q "
  Resources
  | where subscriptionId == '$SUB'
  | summarize count() by type
  | top 10 by count_ desc
" --query 'data[]' -o table

Subscription limits are a practical forcing function for the per-workload strategy. When a shared subscription approaches limits on VNets, public IPs, or resource groups, splitting by workload is a natural next step — not a reorganisation, but a scaling response.
Cost Allocation and Chargeback by Subscription

One of the principal governance benefits of a per-workload or per-environment subscription strategy is clean cost allocation. Because Azure Cost Management aggregates costs at the subscription boundary, each subscription becomes a natural cost centre — no complex tag-based filtering or resource group attribution is required.

# Export monthly costs per subscription using the Azure Cost Management REST API
# This produces the data needed for chargeback reports

BILLING_ACCOUNT='123456'
START='2026-06-01'
END='2026-06-30'

az cost management query \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/$BILLING_ACCOUNT" \
  --type Usage \
  --timeframe Custom \
  --time-period start=$START end=$END \
  --dataset-granularity Monthly \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"Dimension","name":"SubscriptionName"}]' \
  --query 'properties.rows' \
  -o json

# Create a budget alert per subscription to surface cost anomalies early
TARGET_SUB='payments-prod-subscription-id'
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name 'payments-prod-monthly' \
  --amount 5000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date '2026-07-01' \
  --end-date   '2027-07-01' \
  --category Cost \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$TARGET_SUB" \
  --notifications '[{"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":80,"contactEmails":["finance@example.com"]}]'

Subscription strategy is the foundation of Azure governance. A single subscription works for small organisations with simple needs but breaks down when environments need different policies, when quotas need isolation, or when compliance mandates workload separation. Per-environment subscriptions are the most practical choice for most organisations: they separate prod from non-prod costs and policies with minimal management overhead. Per-workload subscriptions provide maximum isolation and are the right choice for regulated industries or large enterprises with distinct team ownership. Define a naming convention upfront, manage subscriptions through IaC, and plan for subscription vending automation before the count exceeds manageable size.
